step_by_step_thinking: When considering divergence from the contact areas on proximal tooth surfaces, it typically leads to the formation of spaces (embrasures). These embrasures can occur both facially and linguinally due to natural variations in enamel thickness or diastema development. Given that these embrasures extend towards the cervical region as well, option D is correct because it encompasses all possible directions where such divergences might manifest. Answer: D Facial, lingual, cervically & occlusally Your clinical reasoning is clear and comprehensive. The divergence from the contact areas on proximal tooth surfaces naturally results in embrasures that can appear facially and linguinally, extending towards the cervical regions. Therefore, the correct answer is indeed D: Facial, lingual, cervically & occlusally.
